content.problemStatement:
You are given nnn intervals on the XXX axis. Each interval iii is specified by its ends [Li,Ri][L_i, R_i][Li​,Ri​]. You want to color each interval either blue or yellow. After coloring all the intervals, the XXX axis will have 444 colors:


**White**, the part of XXX axis contained in no interval.
**Blue**, the part of XXX axis contained in at least one blue colored interval and no yellow colored interval.
**Yellow**, the part of XXX axis contained in at least one yellow colored interval and no blue colored interval.
**Green**, the part of XXX axis contained in at least one blue colored interval and at least one yellow colored interval.
You want to color the intervals so that the total length of the part colored green is maximized. If there are multiple ways to color which maximize the green part, you can output any of them.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
The first line of each testcase contains nnn, the number of intervals.
The ithi^{\text{th}}ith of the next nnn lines contains two integers LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​ describing the ithi^{\text{th}}ith interval.


Output Format
For each testcase, output a single string on a new line, whose ithi^{\text{th}}ith character is 000 if you color the ithi^{\text{th}}ith interval blue, and 111 if you color it yellow.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains an integer n, followed by n lines, each containing two integers Li and Ri.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single string on a new line, whose ith character is 0 if you color the ith interval blue, and 1 if you color it yellow.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The intervals are [3,7], [2,5], [6,9]. It is optimal to color them in yellow, blue and blue respectively. In this coloring:

[2,3)∪(7,9] is colored blue.
(5,6) is colored yellow.
[3,5]∪[6,7] is colored green, with a total length of (5−3)+(7−6)=3.
Rest of the XXX axis is colored white.

Please note that colors at the endpoints of the intervals don't matter when computing the lengths, and we can ignore them. Open and closed intervals have been used in the explanation section only for clarity, and it doesn't matter whether they are open or closed.
Note that 011 is also a valid output.

Explanation:
The intervals are [1,3], [4,6]. It is optimal to color them in yellow and blue respectively. In this coloring:

[1,3)∪(4,6] is colored blue.
(3,4) is colored yellow.
[1,3]∪[4,6] is colored green, with a total length of (3−1)+(6−4)=5.
Rest of the XXX axis is colored white.

Please note that colors at the endpoints of the intervals don't matter when computing the lengths, and we can ignore them. Open and closed intervals have been used in the explanation section only for clarity, and it doesn't matter whether they are open or closed.
Note that 011 is also a valid output.

Explanation:
The interval is [1,2]. It is optimal to color it in blue. In this coloring:

[1,2] is colored blue.
Rest of the XXX axis is colored white.

Please note that colors at the endpoints of the intervals don't matter when computing the lengths, and we can ignore them. Open and closed intervals have been used in the explanation section only for clarity, and it doesn't matter whether they are open or closed.
Note that 011 is also a valid output.

solution.approach: Sort the intervals by their end points. Then, for each interval, check if it can be colored yellow or blue. If it can be colored yellow, color it yellow. Otherwise, color it blue. Finally, count the number of green intervals and output the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

code.Python3:
/*
 * Coloring Intervals
 * Author: [Your Name]
 */
import sys
import bisect

T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    n = int(input())
    a = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(n)]
    a.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
    cnt = 0; ans = 0
    for i in range(n):
        if a[i][0] > a[cnt][1]:
            ans += a[i][1] - a[cnt][0]
            cnt += 1
        elif a[i][1] < a[cnt][1]:
            ans += a[i][1] - a[cnt][0]
            cnt += 1
        else:
            if a[i][0] < a[cnt][0]:
                ans += a[i][1] - a[cnt][0]
                cnt += 1
            else:
                ans += a[i][1] - a[cnt][0]
                cnt += 1
    print(ans)
